storyboard_text:
# Cloud E2E Test – Storyboard  
**Topic:** The relationship between π (pi) and a circle  

---

## 1. Introduction  
| Beat | On‑Screen Intent | Rough Narration |
|------|------------------|-----------------|
| 1.1 | Title slide: “Cloud E2E Test – Pi & the Circle” fades in, background a soft gradient. | “Welcome! In this short video we’ll see how the constant π emerges naturally from the geometry of a circle.” |
| 1.2 | Brief outline appears as bullet points: *What is π?*, *Circumference*, *Area*, *Why it matters*. | “We’ll cover the definition of π, how it relates to a circle’s circumference and area, and why it’s the same for every circle.” |

---

## 2. Defining a Circle  
| Beat | On‑Screen Intent | Rough Narration |
|------|------------------|-----------------|
| 2.1 | Draw a coordinate plane; a point labeled **O** appears at the origin. | “Start with a fixed point O, the center of our circle.” |
| 2.2 | A radius **r** is drawn from O to a point **P** on the circumference; the segment is labeled *r*. | “Choose a distance r – the radius – and mark all points that are exactly r away from O.” |
| 2.3 | The set of points is traced, forming a perfect circle; the circle is highlighted in a bright color. | “The locus of those points is the circle. Every circle, regardless of size, shares this definition.” |

---

## 3. Introducing π via Circumference  
| Beat | On‑Screen Intent | Rough Narration |
|------|------------------|-----------------|
| 3.1 | The circle is “unrolled” into a straight line: the circumference is shown as a straight segment whose length equals the distance around the circle. | “If we cut the circle at one point and lay the edge flat, we get a line whose length is the circumference C.” |
| 3.2 | A ruler appears beside the unrolled line; the length is measured as **2πr** (the formula appears underneath). | “Measuring that line shows it equals 2 × π × r. Rearranging gives π = C / (2r).” |
| 3.3 | The ratio **C / (2r)** is highlighted; a numeric example (r = 1) shows C ≈ 6.283, giving π ≈ 3.1415. | “For any circle, the ratio of its circumference to its diameter (2r) is always the same number – π.” |
| 3.4 | Several circles of different radii appear side‑by‑side; each shows the same ratio C/D. | “No matter how big or small the circle, C divided by its diameter yields π.” |

---

## 4. π and the Area of a Circle  
| Beat | On‑Screen Intent | Rough Narration |
|------|------------------|-----------------|
| 4.1 | The circle is partitioned into many thin, equal sectors (like pizza slices). | “To see π in the area, we slice the circle into many thin sectors.” |
| 4.2 | The sectors are rearranged alternately to approximate a rectangle; the rectangle’s width approaches **r** and height approaches **πr**. | “When we re‑arrange the sectors, they start to look like a rectangle. Its height is roughly half the circumference, πr, and its width is the radius r.” |
| 4.3 | The rectangle’s area is labeled **πr × r = πr²**; the formula fades in. | “The area of that rectangle – and thus the circle – is width × height = r × (πr) = πr².” |
| 4.4 | As the number of sectors increases, the rectangle becomes smoother; a limit symbol shows the approximation becoming exact. | “With more slices the approximation improves, and in the limit the area is exactly πr².” |

---

## 5. Summary & Take‑aways  
| Beat | On‑Screen Intent | Rough Narration |
|------|------------------|-----------------|
| 5.1 | Recap screen: three key points appear with icons – *Definition of π*, *Circumference formula*, *Area formula*. | “To recap: π is the constant ratio of a circle’s circumference to its diameter, leading to C = 2πr and A = πr².” |
| 5.2 | A final animation shows a circle pulsating, with the symbols π, C, and A orbiting around it. | “This simple relationship underlies countless applications in math, physics, engineering, and beyond.” |
| 5.3 | Fade‑out with a thank‑you note and a call‑to‑action for viewers to explore more geometry topics. | “Thanks for watching! If you enjoyed this visual explanation, stay tuned for more bite‑sized math concepts.” |

---  

*End of storyboard.*

beats.narration_hint: Welcome! In this short video we’ll see how the constant π emerges naturally from the geometry of a circle.

args.text: Cloud E2E Test – Pi & the Circle

beats.narration_hint: We’ll cover the definition of π, how it relates to a circle’s circumference and area, and why it’s the same for every circle.
Start with a fixed point O, the center of our circle.
Choose a distance r – the radius – and mark all points that are exactly r away from O.
The locus of those points is the circle. Every circle, regardless of size, shares this definition.
If we cut the circle at one point and lay the edge flat, we get a line whose length is the circumference C.
Measuring that line shows it equals 2 × π × r. Rearranging gives π = C / (2r).
For any circle, the ratio of its circumference to its diameter (2r) is always the same number – π.
No matter how big or small the circle, C divided by its diameter yields π.
To see π in the area, we slice the circle into many thin sectors.
When we re‑arrange the sectors, they start to look like a rectangle. Its height is roughly half the circumference, πr, and its width is the radius r.
The area of that rectangle – and thus the circle – is width × height = r × (πr) = πr².
With more slices the approximation improves, and in the limit the area is exactly πr².
To recap: π is the constant ratio of a circle’s circumference to its diameter, leading to C = 2πr and A = πr².
This simple relationship underlies countless applications in math, physics, engineering, and beyond.
Thanks for watching! If you enjoyed this visual explanation, stay tuned for more bite‑sized math concepts.

segments.text: Welcome! In this short video we’ll see how the constant π emerges naturally from the geometry of a circle.
We’ll cover the definition of π, how it relates to a circle’s circumference and area, and why it’s the same for every circle.
Start with a fixed point O, the center of our circle.
Choose a distance r – the radius – and mark all points that are exactly r away from O.
The locus of those points is the circle. Every circle, regardless of size, shares this definition.
If we cut the circle at one point and lay the edge flat, we get a line whose length is the circumference C.
Measuring that line shows it equals 2 × π × r. Rearranging gives π = C / (2r).
For any circle, the ratio of its circumference to its diameter (2r) is always the same number – π.
No matter how big or small the circle, C divided by its diameter yields π.
To see π in the area, we slice the circle into many thin sectors.
When we re‑arrange the sectors, they start to look like a rectangle. Its height is roughly half the circumference, πr, and its width is the radius r.
The area of that rectangle – and thus the circle – is width × height = r × (πr) = πr².
With more slices the approximation improves, and in the limit the area is exactly πr².
To recap: π is the constant ratio of a circle’s circumference to its diameter, leading to C = 2πr and A = πr².
This simple relationship underlies countless applications in math, physics, engineering, and beyond.
Thanks for watching! If you enjoyed this visual explanation, stay tuned for more bite‑sized math concepts.
